title: Research Hub — One-Time GitHub Setup
text:
Estimated time: 15 minutes. You only do this once. After this, all future publishing is automatic.



Two GitHub repos: 1. research-hub (private) — contains everything in this folder: source .qmd files, configs, the GitHub Actions workflow. This stays private indefinitely — your drafts live here. 2. drmohammadnabhan.github.io (public) — receives only the built HTML output. This is what visitors see at https://drmohammadnabhan.github.io. Before publication, it shows a minimal landing page. At publication, one push deploys the full site automatically.
A GitHub Personal Access Token (PAT) acts as a secure key that lets the private repo push its built output to the public repo. You create it once and store it as a secret.





Go to github.com/new
Repository name: research-hub
Visibility: Private ← important
Do NOT check “Add a README file” (leave everything unchecked)
Click “Create repository”

Leave this page open — you’ll need the URL in Step 5.





Go to github.com/new
Repository name: drmohammadnabhan.github.io (must be exactly this)
Visibility: Public ← must be public for GitHub Pages
Check “Add a README file” (so the main branch exists from the start)
Click “Create repository”

Then enable Pages on this repo: 1. In the new repo, click “Settings” (top menu) 2. In the left sidebar, click “Pages” 3. Under “Source”, select “Deploy from a branch” 4. Branch: main / / (root) 5. Click “Save”




This is the secure key that lets research-hub push to drmohammadnabhan.github.io.

Go to github.com/settings/tokens (or: GitHub → Your profile icon → Settings → Developer settings → Personal access tokens → Tokens (classic))
Click “Generate new token (classic)”
Note: research-hub-deploy
Expiration: No expiration (or 1 year — your preference)
Scopes: Check the box next to repo (this selects all repo sub-permissions)
Scroll down and click “Generate token”
⚠️ Copy the token immediately — it will not be shown again. Paste it somewhere temporarily (Notes, TextEdit) while you complete the next step.






Go to github.com/drmohammadnabhan/research-hub
Click “Settings” (top menu)
Left sidebar: “Secrets and variables” → “Actions”
Click “New repository secret”
Name: DEPLOY_TOKEN (exactly this, all caps)
Secret: paste the token you copied in Step 3
Click “Add secret”

You can now delete the temporary copy of the token from Notes/TextEdit.




Open Terminal on your Mac:
# Navigate to this folder
# (Replace the path below with the actual path to your Nabsidian vault —
#  right-click the Research_Hub folder in Finder → "Get Info" to find it,
#  or drag the folder into Terminal after typing "cd ")
cd "/path/to/your/Nabsidian 2026/Research_Hub"

# Initialize git
git init
git branch -M main

# Connect to the private repo
git remote add origin https://github.com/drmohammadnabhan/research-hub.git

# Stage everything and make the first commit
git add .
git commit -m "Initial Research Hub setup"

# Push — this triggers the first GitHub Actions build
git push -u origin main
When prompted for credentials: enter your GitHub username (drmohammadnabhan) and use your PAT (the token from Step 3) as the password. GitHub no longer accepts account passwords for git push — the PAT is the correct credential.
To avoid being prompted every time, run this once after the push:
git config credential.helper osxkeychain
macOS will store the credential securely in Keychain and you will not be prompted again.





Go to github.com/drmohammadnabhan/research-hub/actions
You should see a workflow run called “Build and Deploy Research Hub” in progress
It takes approximately 2–3 minutes (installs Quarto + Python, builds the site, pushes HTML)
When it shows a green ✓, go to drmohammadnabhan.github.io
Your research site is live

If the workflow shows a red ✗, click on it to see the error log. The most common issues are: wrong secret name (must be DEPLOY_TOKEN), wrong repo name in deploy.yml, or Pages not enabled on the public repo.




Every future update is exactly three commands from the Research_Hub/ folder:
cd "/path/to/Nabsidian 2026/Research_Hub"
git add .
git commit -m "describe what you changed"
git push
GitHub Actions does the rest automatically. The site updates in ~3 minutes.
For pre-publication: the site is visible at your GitHub Pages URL, but since most of your research pages have “Under Review” notices and no raw research data, this is acceptable public presence. If you want the site completely hidden until publication, temporarily replace index.qmd with a “Coming Soon” placeholder — a future Claude session can manage this.
At publication: update the badge-preprint to badge-published, add the DOI to the citation blocks, activate the demo page, push once. Done.




When starting a session to update the Research Hub:

“Update the Research Hub for the COO project. [Describe what to add/change.]”

Claude will edit the relevant .qmd files in Research_Hub/projects/coo/. You then run the three git commands above. Claude does not push to git — you review and push manually, keeping you in control of what goes public.












Problem
Likely cause
Fix




Workflow fails: Error: Resource not accessible by integration
Wrong PAT scope or wrong secret name
Regenerate PAT with repo scope; check secret is named DEPLOY_TOKEN


Workflow fails: remote: Repository not found
Repo name mismatch in deploy.yml
Check external_repository: drmohammadnabhan/drmohammadnabhan.github.io matches exactly


Pages shows “404”
Pages not enabled or branch not set
Repeat Step 2 Pages configuration


Math not rendering
MathJax CDN blocked
Reload page; check browser console


Push asks for password every time
Keychain not configured
Run git config credential.helper osxkeychain

title: Experimental Results
text:
Note



Status: Full results tables and raw data will be released upon paper acceptance. This page summarizes the key findings.

title: Experimental Results
text:
Benchmark Setup
COO was evaluated on six real-world engineering design problems from the CEC 2020 Real-World Constrained Optimization Benchmark (Kumar et al., 2020). Each method was run five times from independent random feasible starting points. All methods used the same Phase-1 feasibility restoration before the main optimization, ensuring the comparison focuses on optimization performance rather than feasibility-finding.
Comparison methods:

COO — this work
PGD — Penalty Gradient Descent using L-BFGS-B on the penalty-augmented objective \(f(x) + \rho\sum_i \max(0, G_i(x))^2\)
PSO — Particle Swarm Optimization with 30 particles

Metrics:

Success Rate (SR): fraction of 5 runs achieving \(f \leq f_{\text{ref}}(1 + 0.01)\) with feasibility — within 1% of the reference optimum
Mean Gap: \(\overline{(f - f_{\text{ref}})/|f_{\text{ref}}|}\) across 5 runs
Gradient evaluations: total gradient calls used

title: Experimental Results
text:
Benchmark Problems



Code
Problem
Variables
Constraints
Character




P1
Three-bar truss design
2
3
Well-conditioned, simple


P2
Pressure vessel design
4
4
Standard engineering design


P3
Welded beam design
4
6
Narrow feasible valley


P4
Tension/compression spring
3
4
Narrow feasible valley


P5
Speed reducer (gearbox)
7
11
Multi-constraint, complex


P6
Himmelblau’s function
2
6
Tight absolute threshold

title: Experimental Results
text:
Key Findings

P1 — Three-bar truss: efficiency advantage
All three methods achieve SR = 100%. COO uses approximately 1,500 gradient evaluations; PGD requires approximately 30,000. COO is ~20× more gradient-efficient at equal accuracy. For problems where gradient evaluations are cheap, this difference is minor. For simulation-backed design where each gradient call involves a finite-element solve or CFD run, the 20× difference is the entire budget.


P5 — Speed reducer: the flagship result






Tip



COO: SR = 100%. PGD: SR = 0%. Penalty gradient descent fails on all five runs.


The 11-constraint speed reducer is the most complex benchmark in the set. Penalty gradient descent aggregates all 11 constraints into a single combined penalty term — the resulting landscape is highly non-convex and riddled with local optima that trap L-BFGS-B. COO’s sequential Snell-KKT reflections handle each constraint boundary as an independent geometric event, decomposing the multi-constraint surface naturally and generating shadow prices for each constraint individually.
PSO also achieves SR = 100% but uses no gradient information and generates no shadow prices. For a practitioner who needs to know which of the 11 gearbox constraints is the binding bottleneck and by how much — the actionable design insight — COO provides this information and PSO does not.


P3, P4 — Honest failure analysis






Warning



COO performs poorly on the welded beam (P3, mean gap ~640%) and tension/compression spring (P4, mean gap ~124%).


These problems have narrow feasible corridors. When the feasible region is a thin valley, sequential Snell-KKT reflections trap the trajectory in a bouncing pattern between opposing walls — the algorithm cannot traverse the corridor effectively. This is an analytically understood structural limitation of the current algorithm, not a parameter sensitivity issue.
COO is designed for problems where constraints are active at the optimum and there is geometric structure to exploit at the boundary. Narrow tunnel geometry violates the Normal Approach Condition (NAC) that underlies the algorithm’s geometric efficiency. For such problems, standard gradient projection or interior-point methods are preferable.


P6 — Parameter sensitivity
COO achieves SR = 0% on P6 (Himmelblau’s function with tight constraints) under default parameters (\(\tau = 10\)). The Ombra fires too rarely and the algorithm exhausts its budget before converging to within the 1% success threshold. Reducing \(\tau\) to 5 addresses this. This is a parameter sensitivity issue, not a fundamental limitation. See Practitioner Guidelines → for parameter tuning guidance.

title: Experimental Results
text:
Cost Profile
COO uses approximately 1,500 gradient evaluations across every problem — flat and predictable regardless of difficulty. This is because the Lumen runs for a fixed iteration budget and the Ombra sub-calls use at most 300 steps. For applications with expensive gradient evaluations, a predictable and budget-bounded method is a practical advantage independent of its accuracy.

title: Experimental Results
text:
Shadow Price Quality
A result with no analogue in the comparison methods: COO generates converging KKT multiplier estimates throughout the optimization run. For P5 (speed reducer), the Luminosity Flux at convergence identifies the binding gearbox constraint — the one limiting the design — and provides its shadow price (sensitivity to relaxation). This information guides the next design iteration. Neither PGD nor PSO generates it.
Theorem 5 establishes \(\|\bar\Lambda_k - \lambda^*\| = O(1/k^2)\) for the averaged Luminosity Flux. The individual multipliers at each boundary event decay to zero as the orbit converges (see Remark 6 in the paper) — the averaging is not merely convenient but necessary to recover the true shadow prices.

title: Practitioner Guidelines
text:
COO is well-suited for constrained nonlinear optimization problems with the following characteristics:
Smooth objective and constraints. \(f\) and \(G_i\) are continuously differentiable, and gradients are available — analytically, via automatic differentiation, or via finite differences. The Störmer-Verlet integrator requires Lipschitz-continuous gradients (Assumption A1).
Active constraints at the optimum. The engineering design has binding inequality constraints — walls that the optimal solution presses against. COO’s geometric insight is most powerful at constraint boundaries. If the optimal solution is in the interior of the feasible set, standard unconstrained Nesterov acceleration (NAG/FISTA) is sufficient and simpler.
Shadow prices needed. You want to know not just what the optimal design is, but why it cannot be better — which constraint is limiting, and by how much. COO generates KKT multiplier estimates throughout the optimization run, not only at the terminal point.
Moderate-to-high dimension with expensive gradients. COO’s \(O(\varepsilon^{-1/3})\) primal rate and flat ~1,500 gradient evaluation budget make it competitive for simulation-based design optimization where each gradient call involves a finite-element solve, CFD run, or experimental measurement.
Multiple active constraints (5–20+). COO handles each constraint boundary independently via sequential Snell-KKT reflections. The 11-constraint speed reducer (P5) is the benchmark showing this most clearly — where penalty gradient descent fails entirely.

title: Practitioner Guidelines
text:
COO is well-suited for constrained nonlinear optimization problems with the following characteristics:
Smooth objective and constraints. \(f\) and \(G_i\) are continuously differentiable, and gradients are available — analytically, via automatic differentiation, or via finite differences. The Störmer-Verlet integrator requires Lipschitz-continuous gradients (Assumption A1).
Active constraints at the optimum. The engineering design has binding inequality constraints — walls that the optimal solution presses against. COO’s geometric insight is most powerful at constraint boundaries. If the optimal solution is in the interior of the feasible set, standard unconstrained Nesterov acceleration (NAG/FISTA) is sufficient and simpler.
Shadow prices needed. You want to know not just what the optimal design is, but why it cannot be better — which constraint is limiting, and by how much. COO generates KKT multiplier estimates throughout the optimization run, not only at the terminal point.
Moderate-to-high dimension with expensive gradients. COO’s \(O(\varepsilon^{-1/3})\) primal rate and flat ~1,500 gradient evaluation budget make it competitive for simulation-based design optimization where each gradient call involves a finite-element solve, CFD run, or experimental measurement.
Multiple active constraints (5–20+). COO handles each constraint boundary independently via sequential Snell-KKT reflections. The 11-constraint speed reducer (P5) is the benchmark showing this most clearly — where penalty gradient descent fails entirely.

title: Practitioner Guidelines
section: When NOT to Use COO (Current Version)
text:
When NOT to Use COO (Current Version)
Narrow feasible corridors. If the feasible region is a thin tunnel (e.g., the welded beam or spring problems), sequential reflections trap the trajectory between opposing walls. Standard gradient projection or interior-point methods are preferable for such geometry.
Equality constraints. The current algorithm handles inequality constraints. Equality constraints can be split into two inequalities (\(h(x) = 0 \Rightarrow h(x) \leq 0\) and \(-h(x) \leq 0\)), but this doubles the constraint count and may degrade performance.
Non-smooth objectives. The leapfrog integrator requires \(L\)-smooth \(f\). For non-smooth problems, use subgradient methods or smoothing.
Global optimality required. COO is a local optimizer — Theorem 4 guarantees an ε-KKT point, not the global minimum. For guaranteed global solutions on non-convex problems, combine COO with a global search strategy: use PSO or a grid search for global exploration, COO for local refinement with shadow prices.

title: Practitioner Guidelines
text:
Parameters









Parameter
Default
Role
Tuning Guidance




\(s = 1/L\)
Auto-estimated
Lumen step size
Estimated from initial gradient difference; rarely needs adjustment. If the Lumen trajectory shows energy drift, reduce by 50%.


\(h\)
\(0.1/L\)
Ombra step size
Keep \(hL < 1\) for stability. Smaller = more stable, larger = faster per Ombra step.


\(\gamma\)
\(0.5\)
Ombra damping
Higher = faster local convergence. If Ombra overshoots near flat minima, reduce to 0.2.


\(\tau\)
\(10\)
APF percentile threshold
Raise to 20–30 if Ombra fires too rarely (too few \(x_{\text{best}}\) updates). Lower to 5 if Ombra fires every few steps but doesn’t improve \(f_{\text{best}}\).


\(C\)
\(50\)
APF cooldown (steps)
Prevents repeated Ombra spawns from the same trajectory region. Increase for high-dimensional problems.


\(N_{\max}\)
\(5000\)
Max Lumen iterations
The primary budget parameter. Increase for problems with many constraints or large feasible regions.


\(N_{\text{Ombra}}\)
\(300\)
Max Ombra steps
Sufficient for most problems under the exponential convergence rate. Increase only if Ombra regularly hits this limit without converging.




Recommended starting configuration
For a new problem, begin with all defaults. Run once and inspect:

How many times did the Ombra fire? (check k_last history) — If fewer than 3 times, lower \(\tau\).
Did \(f_{\text{best}}\) improve after each Ombra? — If not, check that the spawn point is feasible and that \(H_0\) at spawn is indeed low.
Has the Luminosity Flux \(\bar\Lambda_k\) stabilized? — Plot it. If still varying at termination, increase \(N_{\max}\).

title: Practitioner Guidelines
text:
Implementation Checklist
Before running COO on a new problem:

Implement \(f(x)\) and \(\nabla f(x)\); verify gradient against finite differences at a test point
Implement each \(G_i(x)\) and \(\nabla G_i(x)\); verify constraint values at known feasible and infeasible points
Confirm starting point \(x_0\) is feasible; run Phase-1 if needed: minimize \(\sum_i \max(0, G_i(x))^2\) to find a feasible point
Run the built-in Lipschitz estimator to set \(s = 1/\hat{L}\); spot-check \(\hat{L}\) at 2–3 points
Run once with default parameters; check success rate over 5 seeds
If SR < 60%, diagnose: (a) narrow feasible region? (b) APF firing too rarely? (c) Ombra not converging? (d) budget too small?

title: Practitioner Guidelines
text:
Reading the Shadow Prices
The Luminosity Flux output \(\bar{\Lambda} = [\bar\lambda_1, \ldots, \bar\lambda_m]\) contains the averaged KKT multiplier for each constraint.
Interpretation:

\(\bar\lambda_i \approx 0\): constraint \(i\) is slack at the optimum — relaxing it would not improve the objective.
\(\bar\lambda_i > 0\): constraint \(i\) is binding — costing you \(\bar\lambda_i\) units of objective improvement per unit of constraint relaxation.
The constraint with the largest \(\bar\lambda_i\) is the binding bottleneck — the first place to invest redesign effort.

Convergence check. Plot \(\bar\Lambda_k\) over iterations. It should stabilize to a constant vector as the algorithm progresses (Theorem 5: \(O(1/k^2)\) convergence). If it has not stabilized at termination, increase \(N_{\max}\). Instability after 3,000+ steps usually indicates the trajectory has not accumulated enough boundary events near \(x^*\) — check whether the Lumen is spending time near the active constraints.
Note on individual events. Individual multiplier values \(\lambda_i^{(j)}\) at each boundary event decay to zero as the algorithm converges (the approach momentum decreases as the orbit tightens). The average \(\bar\Lambda_k\) correctly recovers \(\lambda^*\) even as individual events become small — this is by design, not a numerical artifact. See the Methodology page for the formal result.

title: Practitioner Guidelines
text:
Citation
If COO is useful in your research or engineering practice, please cite the paper:

Nabhan, M. (2026). Clair-Obscur Optimization: A Hamiltonian Algorithm for Constrained Nonlinear Optimization with Embedded Dual Certificates. Applied Mathematical Modelling. DOI: [pending — will be updated at publication]

BibTeX:
@article{nabhan2026coo,
  title   = {Clair-Obscur Optimization: A Hamiltonian Algorithm for
             Constrained Nonlinear Optimization with Embedded Dual Certificates},
  author  = {Nabhan, Mohammad},
  journal = {Applied Mathematical Modelling},

  note    = {Under review. DOI pending.}
}

title: Practitioner Guidelines
text:
Getting the Code
Source code and benchmark scripts will be released in the GitHub repository upon paper acceptance, with a citable Zenodo DOI. The release will include:

Full COO implementation (Python, NumPy)
All six CEC 2020 benchmark problem definitions
Reproduction scripts for all results in the paper
A minimal working example for a new problem

title: Clair-Obscur Optimization
text:
Note



Status: Manuscript under review — Applied Mathematical Modelling. This page presents the core contributions. Full source code and supplementary materials will be released upon acceptance.

title: Clair-Obscur Optimization
text:
What is COO?
Clair-Obscur Optimization (COO) is a first-order algorithm for solving constrained nonlinear optimization problems of the form:
\[\min_{x \in \mathbb{R}^n} f(x) \quad \text{subject to} \quad G_i(x) \leq 0, \quad i = 1, \ldots, m\]
COO is built around a single mathematical insight: the vector form of Snell’s law of elastic reflection and the Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) first-order optimality conditions are algebraically the same equation. This identity allows the algorithm to extract KKT multipliers (shadow prices) at every constraint boundary contact — at first-order cost, without any augmented Lagrangian, dual update step, or additional tuning parameter.
The name reflects the algorithm’s two alternating phases: Lumen (light — conservative Hamiltonian exploration) and Ombra (shadow — dissipative local convergence). Shadow prices are generated at the boundary between these two regimes, exactly as light produces a shadow at the interface between illuminated and dark regions.

title: Clair-Obscur Optimization
text:
Key Results








Result
Statement
Rate




Primal gradient norm
\(\|\nabla f(x_k)\|^2 = o(1/k^3)\)
Optimal first-order


Primal function value
\(f(x_k) - f^* = O(1/k^2)\)
Nesterov-accelerated


Ombra local convergence
\(f(u_n) - f^* \leq H_0 \cdot r^n\)
Exponential


Global ε-KKT guarantee
\(\exists\, \hat{x}, \hat{\lambda}\) satisfying ε-KKT
Constructive


Dual certificate
\(\|\bar{\Lambda}_k - \lambda^*\| = O(1/k^2)\)
Without dual update



The dual convergence result (last row) is the paper’s primary contribution: true KKT multipliers recovered from the trajectory average, with no additional algorithmic machinery beyond the primal optimization loop.

title: Clair-Obscur Optimization
text:
Sections

Methodology — The Snell-KKT identity, the two-phase algorithm, and the convergence theory
Interactive Demo — Visualize the COO trajectory on benchmark problems (activated at publication)
Experimental Results — Benchmark comparison against gradient projection and PSO on six CEC 2020 problems
Practitioner Guidelines — When to use COO, parameter selection, shadow price interpretation, and citation

title: Clair-Obscur Optimization
text:
Citation






Tip



Citation details will be added upon publication. In the meantime, if you are referencing this work in a preprint or internal report, please use the format below.



Nabhan, M. (2026). Clair-Obscur Optimization: A Hamiltonian Algorithm for Constrained Nonlinear Optimization with Embedded Dual Certificates. Manuscript under review — Applied Mathematical Modelling.

title: Clair-Obscur Optimization
text:
Contact
For questions about this work, please reach out via GitHub or see the About page.

title: Dr. Mohammad Nabhan
text:
Assistant Professor — Industrial & Systems Engineering King Fahd University of Petroleum and Minerals (KFUPM), Dhahran, Saudi Arabia
My research develops optimization algorithms and computational methods for engineering systems, with a focus on constrained nonlinear optimization, convergence theory, and practitioner-ready implementations.
View Research → About →

title: Dr. Mohammad Nabhan
text:
Research

Clair-Obscur Optimization (COO) Under Review
A constrained nonlinear optimization algorithm built on a novel algebraic identity: the vector form of Snell’s law of refraction and the KKT first-order optimality conditions are the same equation. COO generates shadow prices (KKT multipliers) at first-order cost as a geometric byproduct of Hamiltonian boundary dynamics — no augmented Lagrangian, no dual update, no tuning.

Primal convergence: \(\|\nabla f(x_k)\|^2 = o(1/k^3)\) — optimal Nesterov-accelerated rate
Dual convergence: \(\|\bar{\Lambda}_k - \lambda^*\| = O(1/k^2)\) — shadow prices without any dual update step
Benchmark: 100% success rate on 3/6 CEC 2020 engineering problems; sole gradient method to solve the 11-constraint speed reducer

Project Hub →


More projects will appear here as work progresses.

title: Methodology
text:
The paper’s intellectual contribution rests on a single observation: when written in vector form for \(n\) dimensions, the Snell law of elastic reflection and the KKT first-order optimality conditions are the same equation. This is not an analogy — it is an algebraic identity, derived from first principles by imposing two simultaneous requirements on any boundary operator.


At a point \(x\) on the constraint boundary \(G_i(x) = 0\), we require a momentum update mapping incoming momentum \(p^-\) to outgoing momentum \(p^+\) satisfying:
Requirement 1 — Normality (KKT stationarity). The momentum change must be along the constraint gradient: \[p^+ - p^- = \lambda_i \nabla G_i(x)\]
From optimization: KKT stationarity requires the net impulse at the boundary to be normal to the constraint surface. From physics: a frictionless wall exerts only a normal force. Both give the same structural form.
Requirement 2 — Hamiltonian conservation. Total energy is unchanged: \[\|p^+\|^2 = \|p^-\|^2\]
Substituting the first requirement into the second and solving for the scalar \(\lambda_i\) yields the unique non-trivial solution:
\[\boxed{p^+ = p^- + \lambda_i \nabla G_i(x), \qquad \lambda_i = -\frac{2\langle p^-, \nabla G_i(x)\rangle}{\|\nabla G_i(x)\|^2}}\]
This is the Snell-KKT operator. The scalar \(\lambda_i\) it produces is simultaneously the elastic impulse coefficient that reflects the trajectory and the KKT multiplier (shadow price) for constraint \(i\) — generated as a byproduct of enforcing Hamiltonian conservation at the constraint wall.



Lemma 2 establishes four properties of the Snell-KKT operator, satisfied exactly (not approximately):

KKT stationarity: \(\Delta p = \lambda_i \nabla G_i(x)\) — momentum change is normal to the constraint surface
Hamiltonian conservation: \(H(x, p^+) = H(x, p^-)\) — proved algebraically, not approximately
Dual feasibility: \(\lambda_i \geq 0\) when the trajectory arrives from the feasible interior
Complementary slackness: \(\lambda_i = 0\) for all inactive constraints

These are precisely the four KKT conditions, satisfied at every boundary event throughout the algorithm’s run — not just at the terminal point.



The Snell-KKT identity is not an ad hoc construction. It is the conclusion of a four-century trajectory of variational thinking about light-surface interaction: from Heron of Alexandria (c. 60 CE), who showed reflected light takes the minimum total path, through Fermat’s principle (1657), to the modern vector formulation in Born and Wolf (1999). The optimization connection arises because both fields — geometric optics and constrained optimization — derive from the same variational framework.

title: Methodology
text:
The paper’s intellectual contribution rests on a single observation: when written in vector form for \(n\) dimensions, the Snell law of elastic reflection and the KKT first-order optimality conditions are the same equation. This is not an analogy — it is an algebraic identity, derived from first principles by imposing two simultaneous requirements on any boundary operator.


At a point \(x\) on the constraint boundary \(G_i(x) = 0\), we require a momentum update mapping incoming momentum \(p^-\) to outgoing momentum \(p^+\) satisfying:
Requirement 1 — Normality (KKT stationarity). The momentum change must be along the constraint gradient: \[p^+ - p^- = \lambda_i \nabla G_i(x)\]
From optimization: KKT stationarity requires the net impulse at the boundary to be normal to the constraint surface. From physics: a frictionless wall exerts only a normal force. Both give the same structural form.
Requirement 2 — Hamiltonian conservation. Total energy is unchanged: \[\|p^+\|^2 = \|p^-\|^2\]
Substituting the first requirement into the second and solving for the scalar \(\lambda_i\) yields the unique non-trivial solution:
\[\boxed{p^+ = p^- + \lambda_i \nabla G_i(x), \qquad \lambda_i = -\frac{2\langle p^-, \nabla G_i(x)\rangle}{\|\nabla G_i(x)\|^2}}\]
This is the Snell-KKT operator. The scalar \(\lambda_i\) it produces is simultaneously the elastic impulse coefficient that reflects the trajectory and the KKT multiplier (shadow price) for constraint \(i\) — generated as a byproduct of enforcing Hamiltonian conservation at the constraint wall.



Lemma 2 establishes four properties of the Snell-KKT operator, satisfied exactly (not approximately):

KKT stationarity: \(\Delta p = \lambda_i \nabla G_i(x)\) — momentum change is normal to the constraint surface
Hamiltonian conservation: \(H(x, p^+) = H(x, p^-)\) — proved algebraically, not approximately
Dual feasibility: \(\lambda_i \geq 0\) when the trajectory arrives from the feasible interior
Complementary slackness: \(\lambda_i = 0\) for all inactive constraints

These are precisely the four KKT conditions, satisfied at every boundary event throughout the algorithm’s run — not just at the terminal point.



The Snell-KKT identity is not an ad hoc construction. It is the conclusion of a four-century trajectory of variational thinking about light-surface interaction: from Heron of Alexandria (c. 60 CE), who showed reflected light takes the minimum total path, through Fermat’s principle (1657), to the modern vector formulation in Born and Wolf (1999). The optimization connection arises because both fields — geometric optics and constrained optimization — derive from the same variational framework.

title: Methodology
text:
The Algorithm
COO operates by alternating two phases governed by different integrators, with a statistical trigger managing the switch.

Phase 1 — Lumen (Conservative Exploration)
The Lumen phase uses the Störmer-Verlet (leapfrog) integrator — the standard symplectic integrator for Hamiltonian systems. One step with step size \(s = 1/L\) (where \(L\) is the Lipschitz constant of \(\nabla f\)):
\[p_{k+1/2} = p_k - \frac{s}{2}\nabla f(x_k)\] \[x_{k+1} = x_k + s \cdot p_{k+1/2}\] \[p_{k+1} = p_{k+1/2} - \frac{s}{2}\nabla f(x_{k+1})\]
When the proposed position \(x_{k+1}\) would violate constraint \(i\), the Snell-KKT operator is applied to \(p_{k+1/2}\) before the position update. Crucially, \(x_k\) is never modified during boundary handling — only the momentum is reflected. The position update then proceeds with the reflected momentum, keeping the trajectory feasible.
The Störmer-Verlet integrator is equivalent to Nesterov’s Accelerated Gradient Descent at the continuous-time limit (Zhang et al., 2021; Campos et al., 2023). This equivalence is the foundation of Theorem 1’s \(o(1/k^3)\) gradient norm rate.


The Luminosity Flux
Each Snell-KKT boundary event at step \(j\) emits a multiplier \(\lambda_i^{(j)}\). These are accumulated into the Luminosity Flux:
\[\bar{\Lambda}_k = \frac{1}{N_k} \sum_{j=1}^{N_k} \lambda_i^{(j)}\]
where \(N_k\) counts Snell-KKT events (not Householder fallback events) up to step \(k\). Theorem 5 establishes \(\bar{\Lambda}_k \to \lambda_i^*\) at \(O(1/k^2)\).


Trigger — Adaptive Percentile Filter (APF)
The APF monitors the rolling Hamiltonian history and fires the Ombra phase when three conditions hold simultaneously:

Sufficient history has accumulated (\(|\text{history}| \geq 20\))
The current Hamiltonian is anomalously low: \(H_{k+1} < \text{Percentile}_\tau(\text{history})\)
A cooldown period has elapsed: \(k - k_{\text{last}} \geq C\)

The percentile criterion is self-calibrating: it fires when the current state is anomalously close to a minimum relative to recent experience, regardless of the problem’s absolute energy scale. This makes COO robust across problems with very different objective magnitudes.


Phase 2 — Ombra (Dissipative Convergence)
When triggered, the Ombra phase uses the conformal Störmer-Verlet (O-SV) integrator — a geometrically motivated damped integrator:
\[\mu = e^{-\gamma h/2}, \quad v_{n+1/2} = \mu v_n - \frac{h}{2}\nabla f(u_n), \quad u_{n+1} = u_n + h\, v_{n+1/2}, \quad v_{n+1} = \mu\, v_{n+1/2} - \frac{h}{2}\nabla f(u_{n+1})\]
The damping factor \(\mu \in (0,1)\) removes kinetic energy at each step. Theorem 3 establishes the exponential rate \(f(u_n) - f^* \leq H_0 \cdot r^n\) where \(r = (1-\varepsilon h/2)/(1+\varepsilon h/2) \in (0,1)\).
The Lumen resumes from its exact pre-Ombra state after the Ombra sub-call returns — the two phases are fully independent. The Lumen’s Hamiltonian is preserved through every spawn-and-return cycle.

title: Methodology
text:
Convergence Theory
The paper establishes five results, structured as a dependency chain. Each theorem handles one component of the algorithm and cites an independent external reference.









Result
Theorem
Core Reference
Assumptions




Interior gradient norm: \(\|\nabla f\|^2 = o(1/k^3)\)
Thm 1
Chen et al. (2022)
A1, A2, A5


Boundary Lyapunov invariance (segment chaining)
Thm 2
Lyapunov argument
A1–A5


Ombra exponential rate: \(r^n\)
Thm 3
Viorel et al. (2021)
A1, A3, A5


Global ε-KKT convergence
Thm 4
Ekeland (1979)
A1–A5


Dual certificate: \(O(1/k^2)\)
Thm 5
Guminov et al. (2019)
A1, A2, A4, A5




The Key Insight in Theorem 2
The Lyapunov function from Chen et al. (2022), \[V_k = (k+a)^2\bigl(f(x_k) - f^*\bigr) + \frac{1}{2s}\left\|x_k - x^* + \frac{(k+a)s}{2}\nabla f(x_k)\right\|^2\] depends on \(x_k\) and \(\nabla f(x_k)\) — but not directly on \(p_k\). Since Snell-KKT boundary events modify only \(p_k\) while leaving \(x_k\) fixed, they leave \(V_k\) unchanged. The Lyapunov decrease from interior Lumen steps is fully preserved across any number of boundary contacts. This “segment chaining” argument is what allows a single convergence rate to apply to the full algorithm with arbitrarily many boundary interactions.


The Proof Strategy for Theorem 5
The dual convergence proof proceeds in three steps:

Linearize near \(x^*\): reduce the full constrained problem to a local linear equality-constrained quadratic problem near the optimal boundary point.
Import Guminov et al. (2019), Theorem 3: apply the accelerated primal-dual theorem to the linearized problem. Under LICQ (linearly independent active constraint gradients), this gives \(O(1/k^2)\) for both primal and dual averages.
Close the inexact-Uzawa gap: the Lumen performs one leapfrog step per iteration rather than the exact primal minimization the Guminov theorem assumes. This approximation error is \(O(1/k^4)\) per step — absolutely summable — and leaves the asymptotic \(O(1/k^2)\) dual rate unchanged.

title: Methodology
text:
Comparison with Existing Methods









Method
Dual output
Primal rate
Architecture




Gradient projection
Post-hoc only
\(O(\varepsilon^{-1/2})\)
Projects momentum at boundary


Augmented Lagrangian
Explicit dual update step
\(O(\varepsilon^{-1/2})\)
Maintains \(\lambda^k\) as algorithmic variable


Interior-point
At solution only
\(O(n^{3.5}\log(1/\varepsilon))\)
Dense linear systems


COO
At every boundary event
\(O(\varepsilon^{-1/3})\) primal
Measures \(\lambda\) from geometry



The distinction from gradient projection is structural: projection discards the approach momentum (replacing it with the tangent-direction vector), while the Snell-KKT operator reflects only the normal component, preserving the tangential component and measuring the normal impulse as the KKT multiplier. Projection treats the constraint boundary as an obstacle; COO treats it as a measuring instrument.
The distinction from augmented Lagrangian is categorical: augmented Lagrangian multipliers are algorithmic decisions (updated by formula \(\lambda^{k+1} = \lambda^k + \rho c(x^k)\)); COO’s Luminosity Flux multipliers are physical measurements — the elastic impulse coefficients at boundary contacts, computed directly from the trajectory geometry.

title: Interactive Demo
text:
Note



Pre-publication preview. This demo uses simplified 2D test functions to illustrate the algorithm’s behaviour. The full benchmark suite (P1–P6) will be activated upon paper publication.

title: Interactive Demo
text:
Try It
Use the controls on the left to select a test problem and tune the algorithm parameters, then click Run COO to watch both phases unfold. Click Run Both to compare directly against vanilla gradient projection (PGD).

title: Interactive Demo
text:
What the Demo Shows
The demo runs three problems that expose COO’s core strength: reliably finding the global minimum on multimodal landscapes where gradient-only methods get trapped.
Styblinski-Tang — four local minima in \([-5,5]^2\). The global minimum is at \((-2.90, -2.90)\), \(f \approx -78.33\). PGD started from \((2.5,\,2.5)\) converges to a local minimum at \((2.75,\,2.75)\), \(f \approx -50\). COO’s Lumen sweep identifies the correct basin; Ombra then converges to the global.
Rastrigin — approximately 50 local minima in \([-5.12,5.12]^2\), global minimum at the origin. PGD from \((3.5,\,3.5)\) locks into the nearest local basin. The Lumen sweep disperses evaluations across the full domain and identifies the correct basin before refinement begins.
Himmelblau — four equal global minima, all with \(f=0\). This problem highlights a different capability: Lumen illuminates all four basins simultaneously, making COO’s solution independent of starting point. PGD from any corner finds only the nearest minimum.

title: Interactive Demo
text:
The Two Phases (Visual Guide)
☀ Lumen phase — the gold dots are \(N_L\) quasi-randomly sampled candidate points. The orange diamonds are the top \(\lfloor\lambda N_L\rfloor\) beams selected by objective value — the high-luminosity regions of the feasible landscape. This is the Exploration layer.
◑ Ombra phase — the blue trajectory is projected gradient descent launched from the best Lumen beam. This is the Exploitation layer — precision convergence in the correct basin.
The key insight: the Lumen phase costs \(N_L\) function evaluations to select the correct starting point. Those evaluations are cheap because they require no gradient computation. The Ombra phase then spends gradient evaluations only where they matter.
